editorial.overallLabel: Luggage-friendly east Tokyo base with strong airport logic.
watchOut: Less ideal for Shinjuku or Shibuya nightlife
Quieter at night than major hubs
dataSignals: Lower station stress than major hubs
editorial.editorialNote: Oshiage combines direct airport logic with a calmer station-area feel than most central hubs.
editorial.localFeelNote: Residential and practical, with Skytree energy nearby but less chaos than central Tokyo.

editorial.overallLabel: Calmer local Tokyo base near Asakusa without the busiest tourist streets.
watchOut: Some station exits may be less convenient with heavy luggage
Not as famous as Shinjuku or Ueno
editorial.editorialNote: Kuramae is strong for travelers who value local feel and lower crowd stress over big-station convenience.
editorial.localFeelNote: Design shops, cafes, river walks, and a quieter east-side stay.

editorial.overallLabel: Classic Tokyo sightseeing base with strong atmosphere but higher tourist crowds.
bestFor: First-time visitors who want a strong Tokyo feel
watchOut: Some streets are busy with luggage
editorial.editorialNote: Asakusa is excellent for atmosphere, but luggage and crowds matter more than many visitors expect.
editorial.localFeelNote: Traditional, photogenic, and visitor-heavy around Senso-ji.

editorial.overallLabel: Practical east-side hub with Narita access and strong rail connections.
watchOut: Busier and more chaotic than smaller East Tokyo areas
Less polished than Ginza or Shinjuku
editorial.editorialNote: Ueno is not the calmest station, but it is one of Tokyo's most practical first-night bases.
editorial.localFeelNote: Busy, useful, and a little rougher around the edges than polished central areas.

editorial.overallLabel: Cultural east Tokyo base with lower stress and good local feel.
watchOut: Not as direct for airport access as Oshiage or Ueno
editorial.editorialNote: Ryogoku works best when atmosphere and calm matter more than airport directness.
editorial.localFeelNote: Distinctive sumo culture, museums, and a quieter station-area rhythm.

editorial.overallLabel: Quiet, design-forward local base with cafes and museum access.
editorial.editorialNote: Kiyosumi-Shirakawa rewards repeat visitors who want a calm base with cafe culture.
editorial.localFeelNote: Coffee roasters, museums, and tree-lined streets along the Sumida tributaries.

editorial.overallLabel: Lively shitamachi base near Fukagawa, calmer than central hubs.
editorial.editorialNote: Monzen-Nakacho fits travelers who want neighborhood food and a working-Tokyo feel.
editorial.localFeelNote: Sake bars, izakaya streets, and a community-temple rhythm near the Sumida bay.

editorial.overallLabel: Old-merchant central base with easy reach to Tokyo and Ginza.
editorial.editorialNote: Ningyocho gives a quieter central base than Ginza without losing the central rail logic.
editorial.localFeelNote: Old-merchant streets, sweet shops, and the Suitengumae shrine block.

editorial.overallLabel: Compact business-side base with quick Tokyo Station and Tsukiji access.
watchOut: Less character than Ningyocho or Ginza
editorial.editorialNote: Hatchobori is a quiet, useful central base when you value rail logistics over a lively street.
editorial.localFeelNote: Office-side streets that empty in the evening; convenient but unhurried.

editorial.overallLabel: Quiet finance-district base, well connected via Tozai and Hibiya.
bestFor: Day trips to Otemachi or Roppongi
editorial.editorialNote: Kayabacho is a calm, central, slightly underrated base for travelers who don't need a bustling street.
editorial.localFeelNote: Bank towers, river views toward Hatchobori, and unhurried evenings.

editorial.overallLabel: Classic central base with strong rail coverage and Tokyo Station adjacency.
editorial.editorialNote: Nihombashi blends central convenience with a calmer, older-merchant character.
editorial.localFeelNote: Department-store flagships, historic bridges, and a deliberate, central feel.

editorial.overallLabel: Rail logistics powerhouse with high complexity and businesslike surroundings.
bestFor: First or last night in Tokyo
editorial.editorialNote: Tokyo Station scores very high for Shinkansen access but low for simplicity and crowd stress.
editorial.localFeelNote: Efficient and polished, but less relaxed than east-side local bases.

editorial.overallLabel: Polished central base for shopping and Tokyo-Station adjacency.
watchOut: Less local and casual than East Tokyo
editorial.editorialNote: Ginza / Yurakucho balances rail logistics with a cleaner, less chaotic feel than Tokyo Station itself.
editorial.localFeelNote: Department stores, polished side streets, and an upper-end central mood.

editorial.overallLabel: Practical salaryman hub with Yurikamome to Odaiba and walk to Ginza.
bestFor: Quick Haneda routing via Asakusa Line
watchOut: Less polished than Ginza or Marunouchi
editorial.editorialNote: Shimbashi is utilitarian — good rail logic and budget hotels, less character than nearby Ginza.
editorial.localFeelNote: After-work izakaya streets and a busy interchange feel.

editorial.overallLabel: Haneda-friendly central-south base via Tokyo Monorail.
watchOut: Less character than Asakusa or Ginza
editorial.editorialNote: Hamamatsucho is the cleanest Haneda routing option among major central bases.
editorial.localFeelNote: Polished but quiet after-hours, with skyscraper backdrops and the monorail ramp.

editorial.overallLabel: South-side rail powerhouse with strong Haneda and Shinkansen access.
watchOut: Far from Asakusa or Shibuya nightlife
editorial.editorialNote: Shinagawa is unbeatable for rail logistics on the south side — practical, predictable, central-ish.
editorial.localFeelNote: Glass office towers, hotel rows, and big-station predictability.

editorial.overallLabel: Practical Yamanote-side base with Haneda access via Asakusa Line.
editorial.editorialNote: Gotanda fits travelers who want Yamanote convenience without big-station chaos.
editorial.localFeelNote: Office and apartment blocks, river-side walks, and after-work izakaya streets.

editorial.overallLabel: Quieter Yamanote-side base with strong walking access to Ebisu and the river.
editorial.editorialNote: Meguro is a calmer alternative to Shibuya / Ebisu with strong walking access.
editorial.localFeelNote: Riverside cafes, polished residential blocks, and Yamanote-side quiet.

editorial.overallLabel: Polished south-Shibuya stay with strong food and Yamanote convenience.
editorial.editorialNote: Ebisu is a strong second-trip base for travelers tired of Shinjuku-style hubs.
editorial.localFeelNote: Polished food street, Yebisu Garden Place, and a calm-by-Shibuya-standards feel.

editorial.overallLabel: Maximum youth-Tokyo energy with a constantly evolving giant station.
editorial.editorialNote: Shibuya is iconic but tiring on day one — better when you're already oriented.
editorial.localFeelNote: Crossings, screens, packed streets, and a constantly evolving station.

editorial.overallLabel: Maximum big-city energy, but one of the most stressful first-night bases.
watchOut: Arrival with luggage can be tiring
editorial.editorialNote: Shinjuku is powerful after you are oriented, but it is not always the easiest place to sleep first.
editorial.localFeelNote: Energetic, dense, and iconic, with very different blocks around the station.

editorial.overallLabel: Calmer Shinjuku-adjacent base with park-side walking access.
editorial.editorialNote: Yoyogi is for travelers who want Shinjuku access without sleeping inside the Shinjuku machine.
editorial.localFeelNote: Park-side calm, exam-school energy, and after-work runners.

editorial.overallLabel: Big northern hub, slightly easier than Shinjuku for first-time hotel searches.
watchOut: Less iconic than Shinjuku or Shibuya
editorial.editorialNote: Ikebukuro is high-volume but often a better value than the south-side giant hubs.
editorial.localFeelNote: Big-station shopping, anime / character culture pockets, and dense food blocks.

editorial.overallLabel: Practical Yamanote base near Tokyo Station with strong sightseeing pull.
watchOut: Some streets feel chaotic with luggage
editorial.editorialNote: Akihabara is a good multi-line Yamanote base if you don't mind a busy character-culture street.
editorial.localFeelNote: Bright signs, electronics shops, and a strong subculture pulse.

editorial.overallLabel: Compact Yamanote base, one stop from Tokyo Station with budget options.
editorial.editorialNote: Kanda is a quieter, budget-leaning alternative to staying inside Tokyo Station itself.
editorial.localFeelNote: Old offices, izakaya alleys, and a working-Tokyo central feel.

editorial.overallLabel: Underrated east-side base with direct airport routing and walking distance to Akihabara.
bestFor: Narita / Haneda routing via Asakusa Line
dataSignals: Asakusa Line direct to both airports
editorial.editorialNote: Asakusabashi is a quiet, underrated rail-strong base when you don't need a giant station.
editorial.localFeelNote: Doll shops, river walks, and a sleepy weekday rhythm.

editorial.overallLabel: Quiet wholesale-district base with strong airport routing via Asakusa Line.
watchOut: Office and wholesale feel by day
editorial.editorialNote: Bakurocho / Higashi-Nihombashi is a rail-strong, character-light option for value-seekers.
editorial.localFeelNote: Wholesale shops, quiet weekends, and unhurried mornings.

editorial.overallLabel: Big-station east-Tokyo base with Narita rail access via JR.
editorial.editorialNote: Kinshicho is practical and well-connected on the east side, but less calm than Oshiage or Ryogoku.
editorial.localFeelNote: Tower, station, and busy street crossings — a working east-side hub.

editorial.overallLabel: Polished central base between Roppongi and the Imperial Palace.
editorial.editorialNote: Akasaka-mitsuke is a calmer central alternative to Roppongi with strong subway coverage.
editorial.localFeelNote: Side-street restaurants, embassies, and polished office blocks.

editorial.overallLabel: Nightlife and art hub with polished hotels and uneven late-night feel.
editorial.editorialNote: Roppongi works for travelers who want polish and nightlife but can navigate a touristy after-dark street.
editorial.localFeelNote: Embassies by day, neon and clubs by night, art towers above the crossings.

editorial.overallLabel: Polished shopping-side stay between Shibuya and Akasaka.
editorial.editorialNote: Aoyama / Omotesando is a refined alternative to Shibuya for travelers who want calm and cafes.
editorial.localFeelNote: Tree-lined avenues, polished shopfronts, and quiet residential side streets.

editorial.overallLabel: Quieter Yamanote-adjacent base near universities and Kanda River.
editorial.editorialNote: Ochanomizu is a calm central-north base, often forgotten but practical and affordable.
editorial.localFeelNote: Bookstore streets, exam schools, and the historic Nikolai cathedral.

editorial.overallLabel: Multi-line junction with quieter neighborhood feel and good Shinjuku access.
watchOut: Subway-only station block can feel hidden
editorial.editorialNote: Iidabashi quietly offers more subway lines than its low profile suggests.
editorial.localFeelNote: Riverside walks, Kagurazaka's quiet alleys, and an unhurried business-residential mix.

editorial.overallLabel: Quiet university-side base with Tokyo Dome and garden access.
editorial.editorialNote: Korakuen / Kasuga is an under-the-radar quiet stay near gardens and the dome.
editorial.localFeelNote: University blocks, gardens, and weeknight calm.

editorial.overallLabel: Modern bay-side base with new hotels and Toyosu Market access.
editorial.editorialNote: Toyosu trades old-Tokyo character for new hotels and a clean bay-side walk.
editorial.localFeelNote: New apartments, waterfront promenades, and weekend family energy.

editorial.overallLabel: Resort-style bay base for events at Big Sight and Tokyo Bay views.
editorial.editorialNote: Ariake / Odaiba is event-driven — strong when Big Sight is busy, isolated otherwise.
editorial.localFeelNote: Convention centers, waterfront parks, and skyline views across Tokyo Bay.
